title: ufput
text: ufput Copy data from a program buffer into a Uniface field. Explain this code short ufput (unsigned char* field, unsigned char* buffer, short length ); Copy Parameters field Name of a field in your component. To resolve ambiguity, you can qualify the field name with an entity name, for example, field . entity . Instead of the actual field name, you also use the internal bind name as returned by ugetbind . buffer Data to be copied to the field. This data does not have to be zero-terminated. length Number of bytes that should be copied from buffer to field . Use a negative value to indicate that more data is to follow (using additional calls to ufputc ). Return Values Table 1. Values returned by ufput, ufputi, ufputc, and ufputext Value Meaning -1 An error occurred. 0 All data was copied to the field . 1 There is more data to be copied to the field. Occurs when you have specified a negative Length value. Use Library: yrtl Description ufput moves a block of data from a program buffer to a Uniface field. If you intend to move further data to the field, specify a negative length, then make repeated calls to ufputc until you specify a non-negative length. A zero length can be specified to terminate the transfer (see the example below). If you intend to send data that exceeds the field length, use ufputext . Do not use ufput to do this, because it will divide the data into subfields, and insert Gold ; characters at the place where the field ends. Note: If the field data will be stored, exceeding the field length will lead to a store error later on. Moving data into a field using ufput , ufputc , or ufputext is functionally equivalent to a field assignment in ProcScript. This has the following consequences: The data offered should be in the Uniface format expected by that field (depending on data type and/or packing code). The functions $formmod , $formdbmod , $occmod , and $fieldmod will be set according to the rules for field assignments. Trailing spaces will be removed. So if you ufput the string "foo " into a field, the field length in 4GL will be 3, not 6. If you use the ufput , ufputc , or ufputext functions repeatedly for a single field, you should use the internal bind name (returned by ugetbind ) for that field, instead of the actual field name, for more efficient transfer. The format and total length of the data sent via buffer is determined by a combination of the field’s packing code and the character set defined during installation (unless this has been overridden for the entity containing the field). For example, if a field is defined with packing code D1 (indicating a date format of dd-mmm-yyyy ), calls to ufput and ufputc should send a total of 11 bytes if the default character set is DEC ISO Latin-1. For Unicode fields (defined with a W packing code), the field length is based on bytes, not characters. The maximum possible data size is caluclated using the system character set ( $SYS_CHARSET ). For example, if $SYS_CHARSET=UTF8 , a W30 field is treated as a field with a capacity of 120 bytes because one UTF8 character takes up to 4 bytes. When you want to move data to a field including subfield separators or profile characters, you must supply these characters in your buffer. For information about the representation of these characters, refer to the "Subfield Separator and Profile Characters" topic. Example: ufput, ufputc, ufputi, and ugetbind The following example reads a graphics file from disk in chunks of 5000 bytes, and transfers the contents to the Uniface image field IMGFIELD. This field should be defined in the form as: Data Type—Image (database) Widget Type—Picture Interface—R* Note that, in the calls to ufput and ufputc , we specify a negative length, because we are not sure if there is more data until we have read the entire file. Once we have read all the file, we issue a final call to ufputc with length zero to denote that the transfer is complete. Explain this code #include <h3gl.h> #include <stdio.h> EXPORT void GETIMAGE () { FILE* fp; short len; char buf[5000]; char bindname[80]; /* Use bindname for efficiency */ ugetbind("IMGFIELD", bindname); /* Open binary read mode */ fp = fopen("d://temp//mypicture.jpg", "rb"); len = fread(buf, 1, 5000, fp); /* Read first chunk */ ufput(bindname, buf, -len); /* Assume more to come */ while (len > 0) { len = fread(buf, 1, 5000, fp); /* Read next chunk */ if ( len > 0 ) /* Assume more to come */ ufputc(bindname, buf, -len); } ufputc(bindname, buf, 0); /* Tell Uniface it's done */ fclose(fp); /* and close file */ } Copy Related Links Subfield Separator and Profile Characters Uniface uses part of the reserved area of each meta character set (that is, the first 32 characters plus the last character) to store characters with special meaning within Uniface, such as subfield separators and profile characters. ufputext Copy data from a program buffer into a Uniface field, allowing the data to exceed the field length. ufputc Copy additional data from a program buffer into a Uniface field. ufputi Copy data from a program buffer into a Uniface field as if the switch /init were used. ugetbind Get the internal bind name of a Uniface field.
